kjv.2SA_23_3: The God of Israel said, the Rock of Israel spake to me, He that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God.
kjv.2SA_23_4: And he shall be as the light of the morning, when the sun riseth, even a morning without clouds; as the tender grass springing out of the earth by clear shining after rain.
kjv.2SA_23_5: Although my house be not so with God; yet he hath made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things, and sure: for this is all my salvation, and all my desire, although he make it not to grow.
kjv.2SA_23_6: But the sons of Belial shall be all of them as thorns thrust away, because they cannot be taken with hands:
kjv.2SA_23_7: But the man that shall touch them must be fenced with iron and the staff of a spear; and they shall be utterly burned with fire in the same place.
kjv.2SA_23_8: These be the names of the mighty men whom David had: The Tachmonite that sat in the seat, chief among the captains; the same was Adino the Eznite: he lift up his spear against eight hundred, whom he slew at one time.
kjv.2SA_23_9: And after him was Eleazar the son of Dodo the Ahohite, one of the three mighty men with David, when they defied the Philistines that were there gathered together to battle, and the men of Israel were gone away:
kjv.2SA_23_10: He arose, and smote the Philistines until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto the sword: and the Lord wrought a great victory that day; and the people returned after him only to spoil.
kjv.2SA_23_11: And after him was Shammah the son of Agee the Hararite. And the Philistines were gathered together into a troop, where was a piece of ground full of lentiles: and the people fled from the Philistines.
kjv.2SA_23_12: But he stood in the midst of the ground, and defended it, and slew the Philistines: and the Lord wrought a great victory.
kjv.2SA_23_13: And three of the thirty chief went down, and came to David in the harvest time unto the cave of Adullam: and the troop of the Philistines pitched in the valley of Rephaim.
kjv.2SA_23_14: And David was then in an hold, and the garrison of the Philistines was then in Bethlehem.
kjv.2SA_23_15: And David longed, and said, Oh that one would give me drink of the water of the well of Bethlehem, which is by the gate!
kjv.2SA_23_16: And the three mighty men brake through the host of the Philistines, and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem, that was by the gate, and took it, and brought it to David: nevertheless he would not drink thereof, but poured it out unto the Lord.
kjv.2SA_23_17: And he said, Be it far from me, O Lord, that I should do this: is not this the blood of the men that went in jeopardy of their lives? therefore he would not drink it. These things did these three mighty men.
kjv.2SA_23_18: And Abishai, the brother of Joab, the son of Zeruiah, was chief among three. And he lifted up his spear against three hundred, and slew them, and had the name among three.
kjv.2SA_23_19: Was he not most honourable of three? therefore he was their captain: howbeit he attained not unto the first three.
kjv.2SA_23_20: And Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, the son of a valiant man, of Kabzeel, who had done many acts, he slew two lionlike men of Moab: he went down also and slew a lion in the midst of a pit in time of snow:
kjv.2SA_23_21: And he slew an Egyptian, a goodly man: and the Egyptian had a spear in his hand; but he went down to him with a staff, and plucked the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and slew him with his own spear.
kjv.2SA_23_22: These things did Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and had the name among three mighty men.
kjv.2SA_23_23: He was more honourable than the thirty, but he attained not to the first three. And David set him over his guard.
kjv.2SA_23_24: Asahel the brother of Joab was one of the thirty; Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem,
kjv.2SA_23_25: Shammah the Harodite, Elika the Harodite,
kjv.2SA_23_26: Helez the Paltite, Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite,
kjv.2SA_23_27: Abiezer the Anethothite, Mebunnai the Hushathite,
kjv.2SA_23_28: Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai the Netophathite,
kjv.2SA_23_29: Heleb the son of Baanah, a Netophathite, Ittai the son of Ribai out of Gibeah of the children of Benjamin,
kjv.2SA_23_30: Benaiah the Pirathonite, Hiddai of the brooks of Gaash,
kjv.2SA_23_31: Abialbon the Arbathite, Azmaveth the Barhumite,
kjv.2SA_23_32: Eliahba the Shaalbonite, of the sons of Jashen, Jonathan,
kjv.2SA_23_33: Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam the son of Sharar the Hararite,
kjv.2SA_23_34: Eliphelet the son of Ahasbai, the son of the Maachathite, Eliam the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite,
kjv.2SA_23_35: Hezrai the Carmelite, Paarai the Arbite,
kjv.2SA_23_36: Igal the son of Nathan of Zobah, Bani the Gadite,
kjv.2SA_23_37: Zelek the Ammonite, Nahari the Beerothite, armourbearer to Joab the son of Zeruiah,
kjv.2SA_23_38: Ira an Ithrite, Gareb an Ithrite,
kjv.2SA_23_39: Uriah the Hittite: thirty and seven in all.
kjv.2SA_24_1: And again the anger of the Lord was kindled against Israel, and he moved David against them to say, Go, number Israel and Judah.
kjv.2SA_24_2: For the king said to Joab the captain of the host, which was with him, Go now through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan even to Beersheba, and number ye the people, that I may know the number of the people.
kjv.2SA_24_3: And Joab said unto the king, Now the Lord thy God add unto the people, how many soever they be, an hundredfold, and that the eyes of my lord the king may see it: but why doth my lord the king delight in this thing?
kjv.2SA_24_4: Notwithstanding the king's word prevailed against Joab, and against the captains of the host. And Joab and the captains of the host went out from the presence of the king, to number the people of Israel.
kjv.2SA_24_5: And they passed over Jordan, and pitched in Aroer, on the right side of the city that lieth in the midst of the river of Gad, and toward Jazer:
kjv.2SA_24_6: Then they came to Gilead, and to the land of Tahtimhodshi; and they came to Danjaan, and about to Zidon,
kjv.2SA_24_7: And came to the strong hold of Tyre, and to all the cities of the Hivites, and of the Canaanites: and they went out to the south of Judah, even to Beersheba.
kjv.2SA_24_8: So when they had gone through all the land, they came to Jerusalem at the end of nine months and twenty days.
kjv.2SA_24_9: And Joab gave up the sum of the number of the people unto the king: and there were in Israel eight hundred thousand valiant men that drew the sword; and the men of Judah were five hundred thousand men.
kjv.2SA_24_10: And David's heart smote him after that he had numbered the people. And David said unto the Lord, I have sinned greatly in that I have done: and now, I beseech thee, O Lord, take away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly.
kjv.2SA_24_11: For when David was up in the morning, the word of the Lord came unto the prophet Gad, David's seer, saying,
kjv.2SA_24_12: Go and say unto David, Thus saith the Lord, I offer thee three things; choose thee one of them, that I may do it unto thee.
kjv.2SA_24_13: So Gad came to David, and told him, and said unto him, Shall seven years of famine come unto thee in thy land? or wilt thou flee three months before thine enemies, while they pursue thee? or that there be three days' pestilence in thy land? now advise, and see what answer I shall return to him that sent me.
kjv.2SA_24_14: And David said unto Gad, I am in a great strait: let us fall now into the hand of the Lord; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man.
kjv.2SA_24_15: So the Lord sent a pestilence upon Israel from the morning even to the time appointed: and there died of the people from Dan even to Beersheba seventy thousand men.
kjv.2SA_24_16: And when the angel stretched out his hand upon Jerusalem to destroy it, the Lord repented him of the evil, and said to the angel that destroyed the people, It is enough: stay now thine hand. And the angel of the Lord was by the threshingplace of Araunah the Jebusite.
kjv.2SA_24_17: And David spake unto the Lord when he saw the angel that smote the people, and said, Lo, I have sinned, and I have done wickedly: but these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, be against me, and against my father's house.
kjv.2SA_24_18: And Gad came that day to David, and said unto him, Go up, rear an altar unto the Lord in the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite.
kjv.2SA_24_19: And David, according to the saying of Gad, went up as the Lord commanded.
kjv.2SA_24_20: And Araunah looked, and saw the king and his servants coming on toward him: and Araunah went out, and bowed himself before the king on his face upon the ground.
kjv.2SA_24_21: And Araunah said, Wherefore is my lord the king come to his servant? And David said, To buy the threshingfloor of thee, to build an altar unto the Lord, that the plague may be stayed from the people.
kjv.2SA_24_22: And Araunah said unto David, Let my lord the king take and offer up what seemeth good unto him: behold, here be oxen for burnt sacrifice, and threshing instruments and other instruments of the oxen for wood.
kjv.2SA_24_23: All these things did Araunah, as a king, give unto the king. And Araunah said unto the king, The Lord thy God accept thee.
kjv.2SA_24_24: And the king said unto Araunah, Nay; but I will surely buy it of thee at a price: neither will I offer burnt offerings unto the Lord my God of that which doth cost me nothing. So David bought the threshingfloor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
kjv.2SA_24_25: And David built there an altar unto the Lord, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So the Lord was intreated for the land, and the plague was stayed from Israel.

net.2SA_1_1: After the death of Saul, when David had returned from defeating the Amalekites, he stayed at Ziklag for two days.
net.2SA_1_2: On the third day a man arrived from the camp of Saul with his clothes torn and dirt on his head. When he approached David, the man threw himself to the ground.
net.2SA_1_3: David asked him, “Where are you coming from?” He replied, “I have escaped from the camp of Israel.”
net.2SA_1_4: David inquired, “How were things going? Tell me!” He replied, “The people fled from the battle and many of them fell dead. Even Saul and his son Jonathan are dead!”
net.2SA_1_5: David said to the young man who was telling him this, “How do you know that Saul and his son Jonathan are dead?”
net.2SA_1_6: The young man said, “I just happened to be on Mount Gilboa and came across Saul leaning on his spear for support. The chariots and leaders of the horsemen were in hot pursuit of him.
net.2SA_1_7: When he turned around and saw me, he called out to me. I answered, ‘Here I am!’
net.2SA_1_8: He asked me, ‘Who are you?’ I told him, ‘I’m an Amalekite.’
net.2SA_1_9: He said to me, ‘Stand over me and finish me off! I’m very dizzy, even though I’m still alive.’
net.2SA_1_10: So I stood over him and put him to death, since I knew that he couldn’t live in such a condition. Then I took the crown which was on his head and the bracelet which was on his arm. I have brought them here to my lord.”
net.2SA_1_11: David then grabbed his own clothes and tore them, as did all the men who were with him.
net.2SA_1_12: They lamented and wept and fasted until evening because Saul, his son Jonathan, the Lord’s army, and the house of Israel had fallen by the sword.
net.2SA_1_13: David said to the young man who told this to him, “Where are you from?” He replied, “I am an Amalekite, the son of a resident foreigner.”
net.2SA_1_14: David replied to him, “How is it that you were not afraid to reach out your hand to destroy the Lord’s anointed?”
net.2SA_1_15: Then David called one of the soldiers and said, “Come here and strike him down!” So he struck him down, and he died.
net.2SA_1_16: David said to him, “Your blood be on your own head! Your own mouth has testified against you, saying ‘I have put the Lord’s anointed to death.’” David’s Tribute to Saul and Jonathan
net.2SA_1_17: Then David chanted this lament over Saul and his son Jonathan.
net.2SA_1_18: (He gave instructions that the people of Judah should be taught “The Bow.” Indeed, it is written down in the Scroll of the Upright One.)
net.2SA_1_19: “The beauty of Israel lies slain on your high places! How the mighty have fallen!
net.2SA_1_20: Don’t report it in Gath, don’t spread the news in the streets of Ashkelon, or the daughters of the Philistines will rejoice, the daughters of the uncircumcised will celebrate!
net.2SA_1_21: O mountains of Gilboa, may there be no dew or rain on you, nor fields of grain offerings! For it was there that the shield of warriors was defiled; the shield of Saul lies neglected without oil.
net.2SA_1_22: From the blood of the slain, from the fat of warriors, the bow of Jonathan was not turned away. The sword of Saul never returned empty.
net.2SA_1_23: Saul and Jonathan were greatly loved during their lives, and not even in their deaths were they separated. They were swifter than eagles, stronger than lions.
net.2SA_1_24: O daughters of Israel, weep over Saul, who clothed you in scarlet as well as jewelry, who put gold jewelry on your clothes.
net.2SA_1_25: How the warriors have fallen in the midst of battle! Jonathan lies slain on your high places!
net.2SA_1_26: I grieve over you, my brother Jonathan. You were very dear to me. Your love was more special to me than the love of women.
net.2SA_1_27: How the warriors have fallen! The weapons of war are destroyed!
net.2SA_2_1: Afterward David inquired of the Lord, “Should I go up to one of the cities of Judah?” The Lord told him, “Go up.” David asked, “Where should I go?” The Lord replied, “To Hebron.”
net.2SA_2_2: So David went up, along with his two wives, Ahinoam the Jezreelite and Abigail, formerly the wife of Nabal the Carmelite.
net.2SA_2_3: David also brought along the men who were with him, each with his family. They settled in the cities of Hebron.
net.2SA_2_4: The men of Judah came and there they anointed David as king over the people of Judah. David was told, “The people of Jabesh Gilead are the ones who buried Saul.”
net.2SA_2_5: So David sent messengers to the people of Jabesh Gilead and told them, “May you be blessed by the Lord because you have shown this kindness to your lord Saul by burying him.
net.2SA_2_6: Now may the Lord show you true kindness! I also will reward you, because you have done this deed.
net.2SA_2_7: Now be courageous and prove to be valiant warriors, for your lord Saul is dead. The people of Judah have anointed me as king over them.” David’s Army Clashes with the Army of Saul
net.2SA_2_8: Now Abner son of Ner, the general in command of Saul’s army, had taken Saul’s son Ish Bosheth and had brought him to Mahanaim.
net.2SA_2_9: He appointed him king over Gilead, the Geshurites, Jezreel, Ephraim, Benjamin, and all Israel.
net.2SA_2_10: Ish Bosheth son of Saul was forty years old when he began to rule over Israel. He ruled two years. However, the people of Judah followed David.
net.2SA_2_11: David was king in Hebron over the people of Judah for seven-and-a-half years.
net.2SA_2_12: Then Abner son of Ner and the servants of Ish Bosheth son of Saul went out from Mahanaim to Gibeon.
net.2SA_2_13: Joab son of Zeruiah and the servants of David also went out and confronted them at the pool of Gibeon. One group stationed themselves on one side of the pool, and the other group on the other side of the pool.
net.2SA_2_14: Abner said to Joab, “Let the soldiers get up and fight before us.” Joab said, “So be it!”
net.2SA_2_15: So they got up and crossed over by number: twelve belonging to Benjamin and to Ish Bosheth son of Saul, and twelve from the servants of David.
net.2SA_2_16: As they grappled with one another, each one stabbed his opponent with his sword and they fell dead together. So that place is called the Field of Flints; it is in Gibeon.
net.2SA_2_17: Now the battle was very severe that day; Abner and the men of Israel were overcome by David’s soldiers.
net.2SA_2_18: The three sons of Zeruiah were there—Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. (Now Asahel was as quick on his feet as one of the gazelles in the field.)
net.2SA_2_19: Asahel chased Abner, without turning to the right or to the left as he followed Abner.
net.2SA_2_20: Then Abner turned and asked, “Is that you, Asahel?” He replied, “Yes it is!”
net.2SA_2_21: Abner said to him, “Turn aside to your right or to your left. Capture one of the soldiers and take his equipment for yourself!” But Asahel was not willing to turn aside from following him.
net.2SA_2_22: So Abner spoke again to Asahel, “Turn aside from following me! I do not want to strike you to the ground. How then could I show my face in the presence of Joab your brother?”
net.2SA_2_23: But Asahel refused to turn aside. So Abner struck him in the abdomen with the back end of his spear. The spear came out his back; Asahel collapsed on the spot and died there right before Abner. Everyone who came to the place where Asahel fell dead paused in respect.
net.2SA_2_24: So Joab and Abishai chased Abner. At sunset they came to the hill of Ammah near Giah on the way to the wilderness of Gibeon.
net.2SA_2_25: The Benjaminites formed their ranks behind Abner and were like a single army, standing at the top of a certain hill.
net.2SA_2_26: Then Abner called out to Joab, “Must the sword devour forever? Don’t you realize that this will turn bitter in the end? When will you tell the people to turn aside from pursuing their brothers?”
net.2SA_2_27: Joab replied, “As surely as God lives, if you had not said this, it would have been morning before the people would have abandoned pursuit of their brothers.”
net.2SA_2_28: Then Joab blew the ram’s horn and all the people stopped in their tracks. They stopped chasing Israel and ceased fighting.
net.2SA_2_29: Abner and his men went through the rift valley all that night. They crossed the Jordan River and went through the whole region of Bitron and came to Mahanaim.
net.2SA_2_30: Now Joab returned from chasing Abner and assembled all the people. Nineteen of David’s soldiers were missing, in addition to Asahel.
net.2SA_2_31: But David’s soldiers had slaughtered the Benjaminites and Abner’s men—in all, 360 men had died!
net.2SA_2_32: They took Asahel’s body and buried him in his father’s tomb at Bethlehem. Joab and his men then traveled all that night and reached Hebron by dawn.
net.2SA_3_1: However, the war was prolonged between the house of Saul and the house of David. David was becoming steadily stronger, while the house of Saul was becoming increasingly weaker.
net.2SA_3_2: Now sons were born to David in Hebron. His firstborn was Amnon, born to Ahinoam the Jezreelite.
net.2SA_3_3: His second son was Kileab, born to Abigail the widow of Nabal the Carmelite. His third son was Absalom, the son of Maacah daughter of King Talmai of Geshur.
net.2SA_3_4: His fourth son was Adonijah, the son of Haggith. His fifth son was Shephatiah, the son of Abital.
net.2SA_3_5: His sixth son was Ithream, born to David’s wife Eglah. These sons were all born to David in Hebron. Abner Defects to David’s Camp
net.2SA_3_6: As the war continued between the house of Saul and the house of David, Abner was becoming more influential in the house of Saul.
net.2SA_3_7: Now Saul had a concubine named Rizpah daughter of Aiah. Ish Bosheth said to Abner, “Why did you sleep with my father’s concubine?”
net.2SA_3_8: These words of Ish Bosheth really angered Abner and he said, “Am I the head of a dog that belongs to Judah? This very day I am demonstrating loyalty to the house of Saul your father and to his relatives and his friends! I have not betrayed you into the hand of David. Yet you have accused me of sinning with this woman today!
net.2SA_3_9: God will severely judge Abner if I do not do for David exactly what the Lord has promised him,
net.2SA_3_10: namely, to transfer the kingdom from the house of Saul and to establish the throne of David over Israel and over Judah all the way from Dan to Beer Sheba!”
net.2SA_3_11: Ish Bosheth was unable to answer Abner with even a single word because he was afraid of him.
net.2SA_3_12: Then Abner sent messengers to David saying, “To whom does the land belong? Make an agreement with me, and I will do whatever I can to cause all Israel to turn to you.”
net.2SA_3_13: So David said, “Good! I will make an agreement with you. I ask only one thing from you. You will not see my face unless you bring Saul’s daughter Michal when you come to visit me.”
net.2SA_3_14: David sent messengers to Ish Bosheth son of Saul with this demand: “Give me my wife Michal whom I acquired for 100 Philistine foreskins.”
net.2SA_3_15: So Ish Bosheth took her from her husband Paltiel son of Laish.
net.2SA_3_16: Her husband went along behind her, weeping all the way to Bahurim. Finally Abner said to him, “Go back!” So he returned home.
net.2SA_3_17: Abner advised the elders of Israel, “Previously you were wanting David to be your king.
net.2SA_3_18: Act now! For the Lord has said to David, ‘By the hand of my servant David I will save my people Israel from the Philistines and from all their enemies.’”
net.2SA_3_19: Then Abner spoke privately with the Benjaminites. Abner also went to Hebron to inform David privately of all that Israel and the entire house of Benjamin had agreed to.
net.2SA_3_20: When Abner, accompanied by twenty men, came to David in Hebron, David prepared a banquet for Abner and the men who were with him.
net.2SA_3_21: Abner said to David, “Let me leave so that I may go and gather all Israel to my lord the king so that they may make an agreement with you. Then you will rule over all that you desire.” So David sent Abner away, and he left in peace. Abner Is Killed
net.2SA_3_22: Now David’s soldiers and Joab were coming back from a raid, bringing a great deal of plunder with them. Abner was no longer with David in Hebron, for David had sent him away and he had left in peace.
net.2SA_3_23: When Joab and all the army that was with him arrived, Joab was told: “Abner the son of Ner came to the king; he sent him away, and he left in peace!”
net.2SA_3_24: So Joab went to the king and said, “What have you done? Abner has come to you. Why would you send him away? Now he’s gone on his way!
net.2SA_3_25: You know Abner the son of Ner. Surely he came here to spy on you and to determine when you leave and when you return and to discover everything that you are doing!”
net.2SA_3_26: Then Joab left David and sent messengers after Abner. They brought him back from the well of Sirah. (But David was not aware of it.)
net.2SA_3_27: When Abner returned to Hebron, Joab took him aside at the gate as if to speak privately with him. Joab then stabbed him in the abdomen and killed him, avenging the shed blood of his brother Asahel.
net.2SA_3_28: When David later heard about this, he said, “I and my kingdom are forever innocent before the Lord of the shed blood of Abner son of Ner.
net.2SA_3_29: May his blood whirl over the head of Joab and the entire house of his father! May the males of Joab’s house never cease to have someone with a running sore or a skin disease or one who works at the spindle or one who falls by the sword or one who lacks food!”
net.2SA_3_30: So Joab and his brother Abishai killed Abner, because he had killed their brother Asahel in Gibeon during the battle.
net.2SA_3_31: David instructed Joab and all the people who were with him, “Tear your clothes. Put on sackcloth. Lament before Abner!” Now King David followed behind the funeral pallet.
net.2SA_3_32: So they buried Abner in Hebron. The king cried loudly over Abner’s grave, and all the people wept too.
net.2SA_3_33: The king chanted the following lament for Abner: “Should Abner have died like a fool?
net.2SA_3_34: Your hands were not bound, and your feet were not put into irons. You fell the way one falls before criminals.” All the people wept over him again.
net.2SA_3_35: Then all the people came and encouraged David to eat food while it was still day. But David took an oath saying, “God will punish me severely if I taste bread or anything whatsoever before the sun sets!”
net.2SA_3_36: All the people noticed this and it pleased them. In fact, everything the king did pleased all the people.
net.2SA_3_37: All the people and all Israel realized on that day that the killing of Abner son of Ner was not done at the king’s instigation.
net.2SA_3_38: Then the king said to his servants, “Do you not realize that a great leader has fallen this day in Israel?
net.2SA_3_39: Today I am weak, even though I am anointed as king. These men, the sons of Zeruiah, are too much for me to bear! May the Lord punish appropriately the one who has done this evil thing!”
net.2SA_4_1: When Ish Bosheth the son of Saul heard that Abner had died in Hebron, he was very disheartened, and all Israel was afraid.
net.2SA_4_2: Now Saul’s son had two men who were in charge of raiding units; one was named Baanah and the other Recab. They were sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, who was a Benjaminite. (Beeroth is regarded as belonging to Benjamin,
net.2SA_4_3: for the Beerothites fled to Gittaim and have remained there as resident foreigners until the present time.)
net.2SA_4_4: Now Saul’s son Jonathan had a son who was crippled in both feet. He was five years old when the news about Saul and Jonathan arrived from Jezreel. His nurse picked him up and fled, but in her haste to get away, he fell and was injured. Mephibosheth was his name.
net.2SA_4_5: Now the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite—Recab and Baanah—went at the hottest part of the day to the home of Ish Bosheth, as he was enjoying his midday rest.
net.2SA_4_6: They entered the house under the pretense of getting wheat and mortally wounded him in the stomach. Then Recab and his brother Baanah escaped.
net.2SA_4_7: They had entered the house while Ish Bosheth was resting on his bed in his bedroom. They mortally wounded him and then cut off his head. Taking his head, they traveled on the way of the rift valley all that night.
net.2SA_4_8: They brought the head of Ish Bosheth to David in Hebron, saying to the king, “Look! The head of Ish Bosheth son of Saul, your enemy who sought your life! The Lord has granted vengeance to my lord the king this day against Saul and his descendants!”
net.2SA_4_9: David replied to Recab and his brother Baanah, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, “As surely as the Lord lives, who has delivered my life from all adversity,
net.2SA_4_10: when someone told me that Saul was dead—even though he thought he was bringing good news —I seized him and killed him in Ziklag. That was the good news I gave to him!
net.2SA_4_11: Surely when wicked men have killed an innocent man as he slept in his own house, should I not now require his blood from your hands and remove you from the earth?”
net.2SA_4_12: So David issued orders to the soldiers and they put them to death. Then they cut off their hands and feet and hung them near the pool in Hebron. But they took the head of Ish Bosheth and buried it in the tomb of Abner in Hebron.
net.2SA_5_1: All the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron saying, “Look, we are your very flesh and blood!
net.2SA_5_2: In the past, when Saul was our king, you were the real leader in Israel. The Lord said to you, ‘You will shepherd my people Israel; you will rule over Israel.’”
net.2SA_5_3: When all the leaders of Israel came to the king at Hebron, King David made an agreement with them in Hebron before the Lord. They designated David as king over Israel.
net.2SA_5_4: David was thirty years old when he began to reign and he reigned for forty years.
net.2SA_5_5: In Hebron he reigned over Judah for seven years and six months, and in Jerusalem he reigned for thirty-three years over all Israel and Judah. David Occupies Jerusalem
net.2SA_5_6: Then the king and his men advanced to Jerusalem against the Jebusites who lived in the land. The Jebusites said to David, “You cannot invade this place! Even the blind and the lame will turn you back, saying, ‘David cannot invade this place!’”
net.2SA_5_7: But David captured the fortress of Zion (that is, the City of David).
net.2SA_5_8: David said on that day, “Whoever attacks the Jebusites must approach the ‘lame’ and the ‘blind’ who are David’s enemies by going through the water tunnel.” For this reason it is said, “The blind and the lame cannot enter the palace.”
net.2SA_5_9: So David lived in the fortress and called it the City of David. David built all around it, from the terrace inwards.
net.2SA_5_10: David’s power grew steadily, for the Lord God of Heaven’s Armies was with him.
net.2SA_5_11: King Hiram of Tyre sent messengers to David, along with cedar logs, carpenters, and stonemasons. They built a palace for David.
net.2SA_5_12: David realized that the Lord had established him as king over Israel and that he had elevated his kingdom for the sake of his people Israel.
net.2SA_5_13: David married more concubines and wives from Jerusalem after he arrived from Hebron. Even more sons and daughters were born to David.
net.2SA_5_14: These are the names of children born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon,
net.2SA_5_16: Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet. Conflict with the Philistines
net.2SA_5_17: When the Philistines heard that David had been designated king over Israel, they all went up to search for David. When David heard about it, he went down to the fortress.
net.2SA_5_18: Now the Philistines had arrived and spread out in the valley of Rephaim.
net.2SA_5_19: So David asked the Lord, “Should I march up against the Philistines? Will you hand them over to me?” The Lord said to David, “March up, for I will indeed hand the Philistines over to you.”
net.2SA_5_20: So David marched against Baal Perazim and defeated them there. Then he said, “The Lord has burst out against my enemies like water bursts out.” So he called the name of that place Baal Perazim.
net.2SA_5_21: The Philistines abandoned their idols there, and David and his men picked them up.
net.2SA_5_22: The Philistines again came up and spread out in the valley of Rephaim.
net.2SA_5_23: So David asked the Lord what he should do. This time the Lord said to him, “Don’t march straight up. Instead, circle around behind them and come against them opposite the trees.
net.2SA_5_24: When you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the trees, act decisively. For at that moment the Lord is going before you to strike down the army of the Philistines.”
net.2SA_5_25: David did just as the Lord commanded him, and he struck down the Philistines from Gibeon all the way to Gezer.
net.2SA_6_1: David again assembled all the best men in Israel, 30,000 in number.
net.2SA_6_2: David and all the men who were with him traveled to Baalah in Judah to bring up from there the ark of God which is called by the name of the Lord of Heaven’s Armies, who sits enthroned between the cherubim that are on it.
net.2SA_6_3: They loaded the ark of God on a new cart and carried it from the house of Abinadab, which was on the hill. Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, were guiding the new cart.
net.2SA_6_4: They brought it with the ark of God from the house of Abinadab on the hill. Ahio was walking in front of the ark,
net.2SA_6_5: while David and all Israel were energetically celebrating before the Lord, singing and playing various stringed instruments, tambourines, rattles, and cymbals.
net.2SA_6_6: When they arrived at the threshing floor of Nacon, Uzzah reached out and grabbed hold of the ark of God, because the oxen stumbled.
net.2SA_6_7: The Lord was so furious with Uzzah, he killed him on the spot for his negligence. He died right there beside the ark of God.
net.2SA_6_8: David was angry because the Lord attacked Uzzah; so he called that place Perez Uzzah, which remains its name to this very day.
net.2SA_6_9: David was afraid of the Lord that day and said, “How will the ark of the Lord ever come to me?”
net.2SA_6_10: So David was no longer willing to bring the ark of the Lord to be with him in the City of David. David left it in the house of Obed-Edom the Gittite.
net.2SA_6_11: The ark of the Lord remained in the house of Obed-Edom the Gittite for three months. The Lord blessed Obed-Edom and all his family.
net.2SA_6_12: King David was told, “The Lord has blessed the family of Obed-Edom and everything he owns because of the ark of God.” So David went and joyfully brought the ark of God from the house of Obed-Edom to the City of David.
net.2SA_6_13: Those who carried the ark of the Lord took six steps and then David sacrificed an ox and a fatling calf.
net.2SA_6_14: Now David, wearing a linen ephod, was dancing with all his strength before the Lord.
net.2SA_6_15: David and all Israel were bringing up the ark of the Lord, shouting and blowing trumpets.
net.2SA_6_16: As the ark of the Lord entered the City of David, Saul’s daughter Michal looked out the window. When she saw King David leaping and dancing before the Lord, she despised him.
net.2SA_6_17: They brought the ark of the Lord and put it in its place in the middle of the tent that David had pitched for it. Then David offered burnt sacrifices and peace offerings before the Lord.
net.2SA_6_18: When David finished offering the burnt sacrifices and peace offerings, he pronounced a blessing over the people in the name of the Lord of Heaven’s Armies.
net.2SA_6_19: He then handed out to each member of the entire assembly of Israel, both men and women, a portion of bread, a date cake, and a raisin cake. Then all the people went home.
net.2SA_6_20: When David went home to pronounce a blessing on his own house, Michal, Saul’s daughter, came out to meet him. She said, “How the king of Israel has distinguished himself this day! He has exposed himself today before his servants’ slave girls the way a vulgar fool might do!”
net.2SA_6_21: David replied to Michal, “It was before the Lord! I was celebrating before the Lord, who chose me over your father and his entire family and appointed me as leader over the Lord’s people Israel.
net.2SA_6_22: I am willing to shame and humiliate myself even more than this. But with the slave girls whom you mentioned, let me be distinguished.”
net.2SA_6_23: Now Michal, Saul’s daughter, had no children to the day of her death.
net.2SA_7_1: The king settled into his palace, for the Lord gave him relief from all his enemies on all sides.
net.2SA_7_2: The king said to Nathan the prophet, “Look! I am living in a palace made from cedar, while the ark of God sits in the middle of a tent.”
net.2SA_7_3: Nathan replied to the king, “You should go and do whatever you have in mind, for the Lord is with you.”
net.2SA_7_4: That night the Lord’s message came to Nathan,
net.2SA_7_5: “Go, tell my servant David, ‘This is what the Lord has said: Do you really intend to build a house for me to live in?
net.2SA_7_6: I have not lived in a house from the time I brought the Israelites up from Egypt to the present day. Instead, I was traveling with them and living in a tent.
net.2SA_7_7: Wherever I moved among all the Israelites, I did not say to any of their leaders whom I appointed to care for my people Israel, “Why have you not built me a house made from cedar?”’
net.2SA_7_8: “So now, say this to my servant David, ‘This is what the Lord of Heaven’s Armies has said: I took you from the pasture and from your work as a shepherd to make you leader of my people Israel.
net.2SA_7_9: I was with you wherever you went, and I defeated all your enemies before you. Now I will make you as famous as the great men of the earth.
net.2SA_7_10: I will establish a place for my people Israel and settle them there; they will live there and not be disturbed anymore. Violent men will not oppress them again, as they did in the beginning
net.2SA_7_11: and during the time when I appointed judges to lead my people Israel. Instead, I will give you relief from all your enemies. The Lord declares to you that he himself will build a dynastic house for you.
net.2SA_7_12: When the time comes for you to die, I will raise up your descendant, one of your own sons, to succeed you, and I will establish his kingdom.
net.2SA_7_13: He will build a house for my name, and I will make his dynasty permanent.
net.2SA_7_14: I will become his father and he will become my son. When he sins, I will correct him with the rod of men and with wounds inflicted by human beings.
net.2SA_7_15: But my loyal love will not be removed from him as I removed it from Saul, whom I removed from before you.
net.2SA_7_16: Your house and your kingdom will stand before me permanently; your dynasty will be permanent.’”
net.2SA_7_17: Nathan told David all these words that were revealed to him. David Offers a Prayer to God
net.2SA_7_18: King David went in, sat before the Lord, and said, “Who am I, O Sovereign Lord, and what is my family, that you should have brought me to this point?
net.2SA_7_19: And you didn’t stop there, O Lord God! You have also spoken about the future of your servant’s family. Is this your usual way of dealing with men, O Sovereign Lord?
net.2SA_7_20: What more can David say to you? You have given your servant special recognition, O Sovereign Lord!
net.2SA_7_21: For the sake of your promise and according to your purpose you have done this great thing in order to reveal it to your servant.
net.2SA_7_22: Therefore you are great, O Sovereign Lord, for there is none like you. There is no God besides you! What we have heard is true.
net.2SA_7_23: Who is like your people, Israel, a unique nation on the earth? Their God went to claim a nation for himself and to make a name for himself! You did great and awesome acts for your land, before your people whom you delivered for yourself from the Egyptian empire and its gods.
net.2SA_7_24: You made Israel your very own people for all time. You, O Lord, became their God.
net.2SA_7_25: So now, O Lord God, make this promise you have made about your servant and his family a permanent reality. Do as you promised,
net.2SA_7_26: so you may gain lasting fame, as people say, ‘The Lord of Heaven’s Armies is God over Israel!’ The dynasty of your servant David will be established before you,
net.2SA_7_27: for you, O Lord of Heaven’s Armies, the God of Israel, have told your servant, ‘I will build you a dynastic house.’ That is why your servant has had the courage to pray this prayer to you.
net.2SA_7_28: Now, O Sovereign Lord, you are the true God. May your words prove to be true! You have made this good promise to your servant.
net.2SA_7_29: Now be willing to bless your servant’s dynasty so that it may stand permanently before you, for you, O Sovereign Lord, have spoken. By your blessing may your servant’s dynasty be blessed from now on into the future!”
net.2SA_8_1: Later David defeated the Philistines and subdued them. David took Metheg Ammah from the Philistines.
net.2SA_8_2: He defeated the Moabites. He made them lie on the ground and then used a rope to measure them off. He put two-thirds of them to death and spared the other third. The Moabites became David’s subjects and brought tribute.
net.2SA_8_3: David defeated King Hadadezer son of Rehob of Zobah when he came to reestablish his authority over the Euphrates River.
net.2SA_8_4: David seized from him 1,700 charioteers and 20,000 infantrymen. David cut the hamstrings of all but 100 of the chariot horses.
net.2SA_8_5: The Arameans of Damascus came to help King Hadadezer of Zobah, but David killed 22,000 of the Arameans.
net.2SA_8_6: David placed garrisons in the territory of the Arameans of Damascus; the Arameans became David’s subjects and brought tribute. The Lord protected David wherever he campaigned.
net.2SA_8_7: David took the golden shields that belonged to Hadadezer’s servants and brought them to Jerusalem.
net.2SA_8_8: From Tebah and Berothai, Hadadezer’s cities, King David took a great deal of bronze.
net.2SA_8_9: When King Toi of Hamath heard that David had defeated the entire army of Hadadezer,
net.2SA_8_10: he sent his son Joram to King David to extend his best wishes and to pronounce a blessing on him for his victory over Hadadezer, for Toi had been at war with Hadadezer. He brought with him various items made of silver, gold, and bronze.
net.2SA_8_11: King David dedicated these things to the Lord, along with the dedicated silver and gold that he had taken from all the nations that he had subdued,
net.2SA_8_12: including Edom, Moab, the Ammonites, the Philistines, and Amalek. This also included some of the plunder taken from King Hadadezer son of Rehob of Zobah.
net.2SA_8_13: David became famous when he returned from defeating the Edomites in the Valley of Salt; he defeated 18,000 in all.
net.2SA_8_14: He placed garrisons throughout Edom, and all the Edomites became David’s subjects. The Lord protected David wherever he campaigned.
net.2SA_8_15: David reigned over all Israel; he guaranteed justice for all his people. David’s Cabinet
net.2SA_8_16: Joab son of Zeruiah was general in command of the army; Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was secretary;
net.2SA_8_17: Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar were priests; Seraiah was scribe;
net.2SA_8_18: Benaiah son of Jehoiada supervised the Kerethites and Pelethites; and David’s sons were priests.
net.2SA_9_1: Then David asked, “Is anyone still left from the family of Saul, so that I may extend kindness to him for the sake of Jonathan?”
net.2SA_9_2: Now there was a servant from Saul’s house named Ziba, so he was summoned to David. The king asked him, “Are you Ziba?” He replied, “At your service.”
net.2SA_9_3: The king asked, “Is there not someone left from Saul’s family that I may extend God’s kindness to him?” Ziba said to the king, “One of Jonathan’s sons is left; both of his feet are crippled.”
net.2SA_9_4: The king asked him, “Where is he?” Ziba told the king, “He is at the house of Makir son of Ammiel in Lo Debar.”
net.2SA_9_5: So King David had him brought from the house of Makir son of Ammiel in Lo Debar.
net.2SA_9_6: When Mephibosheth son of Jonathan, the son of Saul, came to David, he bowed low with his face toward the ground. David said, “Mephibosheth?” He replied, “Yes, at your service.”
net.2SA_9_7: David said to him, “Don’t be afraid, because I will certainly extend kindness to you for the sake of Jonathan your father. I will give back to you all the land that belonged to your grandfather Saul, and you will be a regular guest at my table.”
net.2SA_9_8: Then Mephibosheth bowed and said, “Of what importance am I, your servant, that you show regard for a dead dog like me?”
net.2SA_9_9: Then the king summoned Ziba, Saul’s attendant, and said to him, “Everything that belonged to Saul and to his entire house I hereby give to your master’s grandson.
net.2SA_9_10: You will cultivate the land for him—you and your sons and your servants. You will bring its produce and it will be food for your master’s grandson to eat. But Mephibosheth, your master’s grandson, will be a regular guest at my table.” (Now Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.)
net.2SA_9_11: Ziba said to the king, “Your servant will do everything that my lord the king has instructed his servant to do.” So Mephibosheth was a regular guest at David’s table, just as though he were one of the king’s sons.
net.2SA_9_12: Now Mephibosheth had a young son whose name was Mica. All the members of Ziba’s household were Mephibosheth’s servants.
net.2SA_9_13: Mephibosheth was living in Jerusalem, for he was a regular guest at the king’s table. But both his feet were crippled.
net.2SA_10_1: Later the king of the Ammonites died and his son Hanun succeeded him.
net.2SA_10_2: David said, “I will express my loyalty to Hanun son of Nahash just as his father was loyal to me.” So David sent his servants with a message expressing sympathy over his father’s death. When David’s servants entered the land of the Ammonites,
net.2SA_10_3: the Ammonite officials said to their lord Hanun, “Do you really think David is trying to honor your father by sending these messengers to express his sympathy? No, David has sent his servants to you to get information about the city and spy on it so they can overthrow it!”
net.2SA_10_4: So Hanun seized David’s servants and shaved off half of each one’s beard. He cut the lower part of their robes off so that their buttocks were exposed, and then sent them away.
net.2SA_10_5: Messengers told David what had happened, so he sent them to the men who were thoroughly humiliated. The king said, “Stay in Jericho until your beards have grown again; then you may come back.”
net.2SA_10_6: When the Ammonites realized that David was disgusted with them, they sent and hired 20,000 foot soldiers from Aram Beth Rehob and Aram Zobah, in addition to 1,000 men from the king of Maacah and 12,000 men from Ish Tob.
net.2SA_10_7: When David heard the news, he sent Joab and the entire army to meet them.
net.2SA_10_8: The Ammonites marched out and were deployed for battle at the entrance of the city gate, while the men from Aram Zobah, Rehob, Ish Tob, and Maacah were by themselves in the field.
net.2SA_10_9: When Joab saw that the battle would be fought on two fronts, he chose some of Israel’s best men and deployed them against the Arameans.
net.2SA_10_10: He put his brother Abishai in charge of the rest of the army and they were deployed against the Ammonites.
net.2SA_10_11: Joab said, “If the Arameans start to overpower me, you come to my rescue. If the Ammonites start to overpower you, I will come to your rescue.
net.2SA_10_12: Be strong! Let’s fight bravely for the sake of our people and the cities of our God! The Lord will do what he decides is best!”
net.2SA_10_13: So Joab and his men marched out to do battle with the Arameans, and they fled before him.
net.2SA_10_14: When the Ammonites saw the Arameans flee, they fled before his brother Abishai and went into the city. Joab withdrew from fighting the Ammonites and returned to Jerusalem.
net.2SA_10_15: When the Arameans realized that they had been defeated by Israel, they consolidated their forces.
net.2SA_10_16: Then Hadadezer sent for Arameans from beyond the Euphrates River, and they came to Helam. Shobach, the general in command of Hadadezer’s army, led them.
net.2SA_10_17: When David was informed, he gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan River, and came to Helam. The Arameans deployed their forces against David and fought with him.
net.2SA_10_18: The Arameans fled before Israel. David killed 700 Aramean charioteers and 40,000 foot soldiers. He also struck down Shobach, the general in command of the army, who died there.
net.2SA_10_19: When all the kings who were subject to Hadadezer saw they were defeated by Israel, they made peace with Israel and became subjects of Israel. The Arameans were no longer willing to help the Ammonites.
net.2SA_11_1: In the spring of the year, at the time when kings normally conduct wars, David sent out Joab with his officers and the entire Israelite army. They defeated the Ammonites and besieged Rabbah. But David stayed behind in Jerusalem.
net.2SA_11_2: One evening David got up from his bed and walked around on the roof of his palace. From the roof he saw a woman bathing. Now this woman was very attractive.
net.2SA_11_3: So David sent someone to inquire about the woman. The messenger said, “Isn’t this Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite?”
net.2SA_11_4: David sent some messengers to get her. She came to him and he went to bed with her. (Now at that time she was in the process of purifying herself from her menstrual uncleanness.) Then she returned to her home.
net.2SA_11_5: The woman conceived and then sent word to David saying, “I’m pregnant.”
net.2SA_11_6: So David sent a message to Joab that said, “Send me Uriah the Hittite.” So Joab sent Uriah to David.
net.2SA_11_7: When Uriah came to him, David asked about how Joab and the army were doing and how the campaign was going.
net.2SA_11_8: Then David said to Uriah, “Go down to your home and relax.” When Uriah left the palace, the king sent a gift to him.
net.2SA_11_9: But Uriah stayed at the door of the palace with all the servants of his lord. He did not go down to his house.
net.2SA_11_10: So they informed David, “Uriah has not gone down to his house.” So David said to Uriah, “Haven’t you just arrived from a journey? Why haven’t you gone down to your house?”
net.2SA_11_11: Uriah replied to David, “The ark and Israel and Judah reside in temporary shelters, and my lord Joab and my lord’s soldiers are camping in the open field. Should I go to my house to eat and drink and go to bed with my wife? As surely as you are alive, I will not do this thing!”
net.2SA_11_12: So David said to Uriah, “Stay here another day. Tomorrow I will send you back.” So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem both that day and the following one.
net.2SA_11_13: Then David summoned him. He ate and drank with him, and got him drunk. But in the evening he went out to sleep on his bed with the servants of his lord; he did not go down to his own house.
net.2SA_11_14: In the morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it with Uriah.
net.2SA_11_15: In the letter he wrote: “Station Uriah at the front in the thick of the battle and then withdraw from him so he will be cut down and killed.”
net.2SA_11_16: So as Joab kept watch on the city, he stationed Uriah at the place where he knew the best enemy soldiers were.
net.2SA_11_17: When the men of the city came out and fought with Joab, some of David’s soldiers fell in battle. Uriah the Hittite also died.
net.2SA_11_18: Then Joab sent a full battle report to David.
net.2SA_11_19: He instructed the messenger as follows: “When you finish giving the battle report to the king,
net.2SA_11_20: if the king becomes angry and asks you, ‘Why did you go so close to the city to fight? Didn’t you realize they would shoot from the wall?
net.2SA_11_21: Who struck down Abimelech the son of Jerub-Besheth? Didn’t a woman throw an upper millstone down on him from the wall so that he died in Thebez? Why did you go so close to the wall?’ just say to him, ‘Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.’”
net.2SA_11_22: So the messenger departed. When he arrived, he informed David of all the news that Joab had sent with him.
net.2SA_11_23: The messenger said to David, “The men overpowered us and attacked us in the field. But we forced them to retreat all the way to the door of the city gate.
net.2SA_11_24: Then the archers shot at your servants from the wall and some of the king’s soldiers died. Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.”
net.2SA_11_25: David said to the messenger, “Tell Joab, ‘Don’t let this thing upset you. There is no way to anticipate whom the sword will cut down. Press the battle against the city and conquer it.’ Encourage him with these words.”
net.2SA_11_26: When Uriah’s wife heard that her husband Uriah was dead, she mourned for him.
net.2SA_11_27: When the time of mourning passed, David had her brought to his palace. She became his wife and she bore him a son. But what David had done upset the Lord.
net.2SA_12_1: So the Lord sent Nathan to David. When he came to David, Nathan said, “There were two men in a certain city, one rich and the other poor.
net.2SA_12_2: The rich man had a great many flocks and herds.
net.2SA_12_3: But the poor man had nothing except for a little lamb he had acquired. He raised it, and it grew up alongside him and his children. It used to eat his food, drink from his cup, and sleep in his arms. It was just like a daughter to him.
net.2SA_12_4: “When a traveler arrived at the rich man’s home, he did not want to use one of his own sheep or cattle to feed the traveler who had come to visit him. Instead, he took the poor man’s lamb and cooked it for the man who had come to visit him.”
net.2SA_12_5: Then David became very angry at this man. He said to Nathan, “As surely as the Lord lives, the man who did this deserves to die!
net.2SA_12_6: Because he committed this cold-hearted crime, he must pay for the lamb four times over!”
net.2SA_12_7: Nathan said to David, “You are that man! This is what the Lord God of Israel has said: ‘I chose you to be king over Israel and I rescued you from the hand of Saul.
net.2SA_12_8: I gave you your master’s house, and put your master’s wives into your arms. I also gave you the house of Israel and Judah. And if all that somehow seems insignificant, I would have given you so much more as well!
net.2SA_12_9: Why have you shown contempt for the Lord’s decrees by doing evil in my sight? You have struck down Uriah the Hittite with the sword and you have taken his wife to be your own wife! You have killed him with the sword of the Ammonites.
net.2SA_12_10: So now the sword will never depart from your house. For you have despised me by taking the wife of Uriah the Hittite as your own!’
net.2SA_12_11: This is what the Lord has said: ‘I am about to bring disaster on you from inside your own household! Right before your eyes I will take your wives and hand them over to your companion. He will go to bed with your wives in broad daylight!
net.2SA_12_12: Although you have acted in secret, I will do this thing before all Israel, and in broad daylight.’”
net.2SA_12_13: Then David exclaimed to Nathan, “I have sinned against the Lord!” Nathan replied to David, “Yes, and the Lord has forgiven your sin. You are not going to die.
net.2SA_12_14: Nonetheless, because you have treated the Lord with such contempt in this matter, the son who has been born to you will certainly die.”
net.2SA_12_15: Then Nathan went to his home. The Lord struck the child that Uriah’s wife had borne to David, and the child became very ill.
net.2SA_12_16: Then David prayed to God for the child and fasted. He would even go and spend the night lying on the ground.
net.2SA_12_17: The elders of his house stood over him and tried to lift him from the ground, but he was unwilling, and refused to eat food with them.
net.2SA_12_18: On the seventh day the child died. But the servants of David were afraid to inform him that the child had died, for they said, “While the child was still alive he would not listen to us when we spoke to him. How can we tell him that the child is dead? He will do himself harm!”
net.2SA_12_19: When David saw that his servants were whispering to one another, he realized that the child was dead. So David asked his servants, “Is the child dead?” They replied, “Yes, he’s dead.”
net.2SA_12_20: So David got up from the ground, bathed, put on oil, and changed his clothes. He went to the house of the Lord and worshiped. Then, when he entered his palace, he requested that food be brought to him, and he ate.
net.2SA_12_21: His servants said to him, “What is this that you have done? While the child was still alive, you fasted and wept. Once the child was dead you got up and ate food!”
net.2SA_12_22: He replied, “While the child was still alive, I fasted and wept because I thought, ‘Perhaps the Lord will show pity and the child will live.’
net.2SA_12_23: But now he is dead. Why should I fast? Am I able to bring him back at this point? I will go to him, but he cannot return to me!”
net.2SA_12_24: So David comforted his wife Bathsheba. He came to her and went to bed with her. Later she gave birth to a son, and David named him Solomon. Now the Lord loved the child
net.2SA_12_25: and sent word through Nathan the prophet that he should be named Jedidiah for the Lord’s sake. David’s Forces Defeat the Ammonites
net.2SA_12_26: So Joab fought against Rabbah of the Ammonites and captured the royal city.
net.2SA_12_27: Joab then sent messengers to David, saying, “I have fought against Rabbah and have captured the water supply of the city.
net.2SA_12_28: So now assemble the rest of the army and besiege the city and capture it. Otherwise I will capture the city and it will be named for me.”
net.2SA_12_29: So David assembled all the army and went to Rabbah and fought against it and captured it.
net.2SA_12_30: He took the crown of their king from his head—it was gold, weighed about seventy-five pounds, and held a precious stone—and it was placed on David’s head. He also took from the city a great deal of plunder.
net.2SA_12_31: He removed the people who were in it and made them labor with saws, iron picks, and iron axes, putting them to work at the brick kiln. This was his policy with all the Ammonite cities. Then David and all the army returned to Jerusalem.
net.2SA_13_1: Now David’s son Absalom had a beautiful sister named Tamar. In the course of time David’s son Amnon fell madly in love with her.
net.2SA_13_2: But Amnon became frustrated because he was so lovesick over his sister Tamar. For she was a virgin, and to Amnon it seemed out of the question to do anything to her.
net.2SA_13_3: Now Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, the son of David’s brother Shimeah. Jonadab was a very crafty man.
net.2SA_13_4: He asked Amnon, “Why are you, the king’s son, so depressed every morning? Can’t you tell me?” So Amnon said to him, “I’m in love with Tamar the sister of my brother Absalom.”
net.2SA_13_5: Jonadab replied to him, “Lie down on your bed and pretend to be sick. When your father comes in to see you, say to him, ‘Please let my sister Tamar come in so she can fix some food for me. Let her prepare the food in my sight so I can watch. Then I will eat from her hand.’”
net.2SA_13_6: So Amnon lay down and pretended to be sick. When the king came in to see him, Amnon said to the king, “Please let my sister Tamar come in so she can make a couple of cakes in my sight. Then I will eat from her hand.”
net.2SA_13_7: So David sent Tamar to the house saying, “Please go to the house of Amnon your brother and prepare some food for him.”
net.2SA_13_8: So Tamar went to the house of Amnon her brother, who was lying down. She took the dough, kneaded it, made some cakes while he watched, and baked them.
net.2SA_13_9: But when she took the pan and set it before him, he refused to eat. Instead Amnon said, “Get everyone out of here!” So everyone left.
net.2SA_13_10: Then Amnon said to Tamar, “Bring the cakes into the bedroom; then I will eat from your hand.” So Tamar took the cakes that she had prepared and brought them to her brother Amnon in the bedroom.
net.2SA_13_11: As she brought them to him to eat, he grabbed her and said to her, “Come on! Get in bed with me, my sister!”
net.2SA_13_12: But she said to him, “No, my brother! Don’t humiliate me! This just isn’t done in Israel! Don’t do this foolish thing!
net.2SA_13_13: How could I ever be rid of my humiliation? And you would be considered one of the fools in Israel! Just speak to the king, for he will not withhold me from you.”
net.2SA_13_14: But he refused to listen to her. He overpowered her and humiliated her by raping her.
net.2SA_13_15: Then Amnon greatly despised her. His disdain toward her surpassed the love he had previously felt toward her. Amnon said to her, “Get up and leave!”
net.2SA_13_16: But she said to him, “No I won’t, for sending me away now would be worse than what you did to me earlier!” But he refused to listen to her.
net.2SA_13_17: He called his personal attendant and said to him, “Take this woman out of my sight and lock the door behind her!”
net.2SA_13_18: (Now she was wearing a long robe, for this is what the king’s virgin daughters used to wear.) So Amnon’s attendant removed her and bolted the door behind her.
net.2SA_13_19: Then Tamar put ashes on her head and tore the long robe she was wearing. She put her hands on her head and went on her way, wailing as she went.
net.2SA_13_20: Her brother Absalom said to her, “Was Amnon your brother with you? Now be quiet, my sister. He is your brother. Don’t take it so seriously!” Tamar, devastated, lived in the house of her brother Absalom.
net.2SA_13_21: Now King David heard about all these things and was very angry.
net.2SA_13_22: But Absalom said nothing to Amnon, either bad or good, yet Absalom hated Amnon because he had humiliated his sister Tamar. Absalom Has Amnon Put to Death
net.2SA_13_23: Two years later Absalom’s sheepshearers were in Baal Hazor, near Ephraim. Absalom invited all the king’s sons.
net.2SA_13_24: Then Absalom went to the king and said, “My shearers have begun their work. Let the king and his servants go with me.”
net.2SA_13_25: But the king said to Absalom, “No, my son. We shouldn’t all go. We shouldn’t burden you in that way.” Though Absalom pressed him, the king was not willing to go. Instead, David blessed him.
net.2SA_13_26: Then Absalom said, “If you will not go, then let my brother Amnon go with us.” The king replied to him, “Why should he go with you?”
net.2SA_13_27: But when Absalom pressed him, he sent Amnon and all the king’s sons along with him.
net.2SA_13_28: Absalom instructed his servants, “Look! When Amnon is drunk and I say to you, ‘Strike Amnon down,’ kill him then and there. Don’t fear! Is it not I who have given you these instructions? Be strong and courageous!”
net.2SA_13_29: So Absalom’s servants did to Amnon exactly what Absalom had instructed. Then all the king’s sons got up; each one rode away on his mule and fled.
net.2SA_13_30: While they were still on their way, the following report reached David: “Absalom has killed all the king’s sons; not one of them is left!”
net.2SA_13_31: Then the king stood up and tore his garments and lay down on the ground. All his servants were standing there with torn garments as well.
net.2SA_13_32: Jonadab, the son of David’s brother Shimeah, said, “My lord should not say, ‘They have killed all the young men who are the king’s sons.’ For only Amnon is dead. This is what Absalom has talked about from the day that Amnon humiliated his sister Tamar.
net.2SA_13_33: Now don’t let my lord the king be concerned about the report that has come saying, ‘All the king’s sons are dead.’ It is only Amnon who is dead.”
net.2SA_13_34: In the meantime Absalom fled. When the servant who was the watchman looked up, he saw many people coming from the west on a road beside the hill.
net.2SA_13_35: Jonadab said to the king, “Look! The king’s sons have come! It’s just as I said.”
net.2SA_13_36: Just as he finished speaking, the king’s sons arrived, wailing and weeping. The king and all his servants wept loudly as well.
net.2SA_13_37: But Absalom fled and went to King Talmai son of Ammihud of Geshur. And David grieved over his son every day.
net.2SA_13_38: After Absalom fled and went to Geshur, he remained there for three years.
net.2SA_13_39: The king longed to go to Absalom, for he had since been consoled over the death of Amnon.
net.2SA_14_1: Now Joab son of Zeruiah realized that the king longed to see Absalom.
net.2SA_14_2: So Joab sent to Tekoa and brought from there a wise woman. He told her, “Pretend to be in mourning and put on garments for mourning. Don’t anoint yourself with oil. Instead, act like a woman who has been mourning for the dead for some time.
net.2SA_14_3: Go to the king and speak to him in the following fashion.” Then Joab told her what to say.
net.2SA_14_4: So the Tekoan woman went to the king. She bowed down with her face to the ground in deference to him and said, “Please help me, O king!”
net.2SA_14_5: The king replied to her, “What do you want?” She answered, “I am a widow; my husband is dead.
net.2SA_14_6: Your servant has two sons. When the two of them got into a fight in the field, there was no one present who could intervene. One of them struck the other and killed him.
net.2SA_14_7: Now the entire family has risen up against your servant, saying, ‘Turn over the one who struck down his brother, so that we can execute him and avenge the death of his brother whom he killed. In so doing we will also destroy the heir.’ They want to extinguish my remaining coal, leaving no one on the face of the earth to carry on the name of my husband.”
net.2SA_14_8: Then the king told the woman, “Go to your home. I will give instructions concerning your situation.”
net.2SA_14_9: The Tekoan woman said to the king, “My lord the king, let any blame fall on me and on the house of my father. But let the king and his throne be innocent!”
net.2SA_14_10: The king said, “Bring to me whoever speaks to you, and he won’t bother you again!”
net.2SA_14_11: She replied, “In that case, let the king invoke the name of the Lord your God so that the avenger of blood may not add to the killing! Then they will not destroy my son!” He replied, “As surely as the Lord lives, not a single hair of your son’s head will fall to the ground.”
net.2SA_14_12: Then the woman said, “Please permit your servant to speak to my lord the king about another matter.” He replied, “Tell me.”
net.2SA_14_13: The woman said, “Why have you devised something like this against God’s people? When the king speaks in this fashion, he makes himself guilty, for the king has not brought back the one he has banished.
net.2SA_14_14: Certainly we must die, and are like water spilled on the ground that cannot be gathered up again. But God does not take away life; instead he devises ways for the banished to be restored.
net.2SA_14_15: I have now come to speak with my lord the king about this matter, because the people have made me fearful. But your servant said, ‘I will speak to the king! Perhaps the king will do what his female servant asks.
net.2SA_14_16: Yes! The king may listen and deliver his female servant from the hand of the man who seeks to remove both me and my son from the inheritance God has given us!’
net.2SA_14_17: So your servant said, ‘May the word of my lord the king be my security, for my lord the king is like the angel of God when it comes to deciding between right and wrong! May the Lord your God be with you!’”
net.2SA_14_18: Then the king replied to the woman, “Don’t hide any information from me when I question you.” The woman said, “Let my lord the king speak.”
net.2SA_14_19: The king said, “Did Joab put you up to all of this?” The woman answered, “As surely as you live, my lord the king, there is no deviation to the right or to the left from all that my lord the king has said. For your servant Joab gave me instructions. He has put all these words in your servant’s mouth.
net.2SA_14_20: Your servant Joab did this so as to change this situation. But my lord has wisdom like that of the angel of God, and knows everything that is happening in the land.”
net.2SA_14_21: Then the king said to Joab, “All right! I will do this thing. Go and bring back the young man Absalom!”
net.2SA_14_22: Then Joab bowed down with his face toward the ground and thanked the king. Joab said, “Today your servant knows that I have found favor in your sight, my lord the king, because the king has granted the request of your servant!”
net.2SA_14_23: So Joab got up and went to Geshur and brought Absalom back to Jerusalem.
net.2SA_14_24: But the king said, “Let him go over to his own house. He may not see my face.” So Absalom went over to his own house; he did not see the king’s face.
net.2SA_14_25: Now in all Israel everyone acknowledged that there was no man as handsome as Absalom. From the soles of his feet to the top of his head he was perfect in appearance.
net.2SA_14_26: When he would shave his head—at the end of every year he used to shave his head, for it grew too long and he would shave it—he used to weigh the hair of his head at three pounds according to the king’s weight.
net.2SA_14_27: Absalom had three sons and one daughter, whose name was Tamar. She was a very attractive woman.
net.2SA_14_28: Absalom lived in Jerusalem for two years without seeing the king’s face.
net.2SA_14_29: Then Absalom sent a message to Joab asking him to send him to the king, but Joab was not willing to come to him. So he sent a second message to him, but he still was not willing to come.
net.2SA_14_30: So he said to his servants, “Look, Joab has a portion of field adjacent to mine and he has some barley there. Go and set it on fire.” So Absalom’s servants set Joab’s portion of the field on fire.
net.2SA_14_31: Then Joab got up and came to Absalom’s house. He said to him, “Why did your servants set my portion of field on fire?”
net.2SA_14_32: Absalom said to Joab, “Look, I sent a message to you saying, ‘Come here so that I can send you to the king with this message: “Why have I come from Geshur? It would be better for me if I were still there.”’ Let me now see the face of the king. If I am at fault, let him put me to death!”
net.2SA_14_33: So Joab went to the king and informed him. The king summoned Absalom, and he came to the king. Absalom bowed down before the king with his face toward the ground and the king kissed him.
net.2SA_15_1: Some time later Absalom managed to acquire a chariot and horses, as well as fifty men to serve as his royal guard.
net.2SA_15_2: Now Absalom used to get up early and stand beside the road that led to the city gate. Whenever anyone came by who had a complaint to bring to the king for arbitration, Absalom would call out to him, “What city are you from?” The person would answer, “I, your servant, am from one of the tribes of Israel.”
net.2SA_15_3: Absalom would then say to him, “Look, your claims are legitimate and appropriate. But there is no representative of the king who will listen to you.”
net.2SA_15_4: Absalom would then say, “If only they would make me a judge in the land! Then everyone who had a judicial complaint could come to me and I would make sure he receives a just settlement.”
net.2SA_15_5: When someone approached to bow before him, Absalom would extend his hand and embrace him and kiss him.
net.2SA_15_6: Absalom acted this way toward everyone in Israel who came to the king for justice. In this way Absalom won the loyalty of the citizens of Israel.
net.2SA_15_7: After four years Absalom said to the king, “Let me go and repay my vow that I made to the Lord while I was in Hebron.
net.2SA_15_8: For I made this vow when I was living in Geshur in Aram: ‘If the Lord really does allow me to return to Jerusalem, I will serve the Lord.’”
net.2SA_15_9: The king replied to him, “Go in peace.” So Absalom got up and went to Hebron.
net.2SA_15_10: Then Absalom sent spies through all the tribes of Israel who said, “When you hear the sound of the horn, you may assume that Absalom rules in Hebron.”
net.2SA_15_11: Now 200 men had gone with Absalom from Jerusalem. Since they were invited, they went naively and were unaware of what Absalom was planning.
net.2SA_15_12: While he was offering sacrifices, Absalom sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite, David’s adviser, to come from his city, Giloh. The conspiracy was gaining momentum, and the people were starting to side with Absalom. David Flees from Jerusalem
net.2SA_15_13: Then a messenger came to David and reported, “The men of Israel are loyal to Absalom!”
net.2SA_15_14: So David said to all his servants who were with him in Jerusalem, “Come on! Let’s escape! Otherwise no one will be delivered from Absalom! Go immediately, or else he will quickly overtake us and bring disaster on us and kill the city’s residents with the sword.”
net.2SA_15_15: The king’s servants replied to the king, “We will do whatever our lord the king decides.”
net.2SA_15_16: So the king and all the members of his royal court set out on foot, though the king left behind ten concubines to attend to the palace.
net.2SA_15_17: The king and all the people set out on foot, pausing at a spot some distance away.
net.2SA_15_18: All his servants were leaving with him, along with all the Kerethites, all the Pelethites, and all the Gittites—some 600 men who had come on foot from Gath. They were leaving with the king.
net.2SA_15_19: Then the king said to Ittai the Gittite, “Why should you come with us? Go back and stay with the new king, for you are a foreigner and an exile from your own country.
net.2SA_15_20: It seems as if you arrived just yesterday. Today should I make you wander around by going with us? I go where I must go. But as for you, go back and take your men with you. May genuine loyal love protect you!”
net.2SA_15_21: But Ittai replied to the king, “As surely as the Lord lives and as my lord the king lives, wherever my lord the king is, whether it means death or life, there I will be as well!”
net.2SA_15_22: So David said to Ittai, “Come along then.” So Ittai the Gittite went along, accompanied by all his men and all the dependents who were with him.
net.2SA_15_23: All the land was weeping loudly as all these people were leaving. As the king was crossing over the Kidron Valley, all the people were leaving on the road that leads to the desert.
net.2SA_15_24: Zadok and all the Levites who were with him were carrying the ark of the covenant of God. When they positioned the ark of God, Abiathar offered sacrifices until all the people had finished leaving the city.
net.2SA_15_25: Then the king said to Zadok, “Take the ark of God back to the city. If I find favor in the Lord’s sight he will bring me back and enable me to see both it and his dwelling place again.
net.2SA_15_26: However, if he should say, ‘I do not take pleasure in you,’ then he will deal with me in a way that he considers appropriate.”
net.2SA_15_27: The king said to Zadok the priest, “Are you a seer? Go back to the city in peace! Your son Ahimaaz and Abiathar’s son Jonathan may go with you and Abiathar.
net.2SA_15_28: Look, I will be waiting at the fords of the desert until word from you reaches me.”
net.2SA_15_29: So Zadok and Abiathar took the ark of God back to Jerusalem and remained there.
net.2SA_15_30: As David was going up the Mount of Olives, he was weeping as he went; his head was covered and his feet were bare. All the people who were with him also had their heads covered and were weeping as they went up.
net.2SA_15_31: Now David had been told, “Ahithophel has sided with the conspirators who are with Absalom.” So David prayed, “Make the advice of Ahithophel foolish, O Lord.”
net.2SA_15_32: When David reached the summit, where he used to worship God, Hushai the Arkite met him with his clothes torn and dirt on his head.
net.2SA_15_33: David said to him, “If you leave with me you will be a burden to me.
net.2SA_15_34: But you will be able to counter the advice of Ahithophel if you go back to the city and say to Absalom, ‘I will be your servant, O king! Previously I was your father’s servant, and now I will be your servant.’
net.2SA_15_35: Zadok and Abiathar the priests will be there with you. Everything you hear in the king’s palace you must tell Zadok and Abiathar the priests.
net.2SA_15_36: Furthermore, their two sons are there with them, Zadok’s son Ahimaaz and Abiathar’s son Jonathan. You must send them to me with any information you hear.”
net.2SA_15_37: So David’s friend Hushai arrived in the city, just as Absalom was entering Jerusalem.
net.2SA_16_1: When David had gone a short way beyond the summit, Ziba the servant of Mephibosheth was there to meet him. He had a couple of donkeys that were saddled, and on them were 200 loaves of bread, 100 raisin cakes, 100 baskets of summer fruit, and a container of wine.
net.2SA_16_2: The king asked Ziba, “Why did you bring these things?” Ziba replied, “The donkeys are for the king’s family to ride on, the loaves of bread and the summer fruit are for the attendants to eat, and the wine is for those who get exhausted in the desert.”
net.2SA_16_3: The king asked, “Where is your master’s grandson?” Ziba replied to the king, “He remains in Jerusalem, for he said, ‘Today the house of Israel will give back to me my grandfather’s kingdom.’”
net.2SA_16_4: The king said to Ziba, “Everything that was Mephibosheth’s now belongs to you.” Ziba replied, “I bow before you. May I find favor in your sight, my lord the king.” Shimei Curses David and His Men
net.2SA_16_5: Then King David reached Bahurim. There a man from Saul’s extended family named Shimei son of Gera came out, yelling curses as he approached.
net.2SA_16_6: He threw stones at David and all of King David’s servants, as well as all the people and the soldiers who were on his right and on his left.
net.2SA_16_7: As he yelled curses, Shimei said, “Leave! Leave! You man of bloodshed, you wicked man!
net.2SA_16_8: The Lord has punished you for all the spilled blood of the house of Saul, in whose place you rule. Now the Lord has given the kingdom into the hand of your son Absalom. Disaster has overtaken you, for you are a man of bloodshed!”
net.2SA_16_9: Then Abishai son of Zeruiah said to the king, “Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? Let me go over and cut off his head!”
net.2SA_16_10: But the king said, “What do we have in common, you sons of Zeruiah? If he curses because the Lord has said to him, ‘Curse David!,’ who can say to him, ‘Why have you done this?’”
net.2SA_16_11: Then David said to Abishai and to all his servants, “My own son, my very own flesh and blood, is trying to take my life. So also now this Benjaminite! Leave him alone so that he can curse, for the Lord has spoken to him.
net.2SA_16_12: Perhaps the Lord will notice my affliction and this day grant me good in place of his curse.”
net.2SA_16_13: So David and his men went on their way. But Shimei kept going along the side of the hill opposite him, yelling curses as he threw stones and dirt at them.
net.2SA_16_14: The king and all the people who were with him arrived exhausted at their destination, where David refreshed himself. The Advice of Ahithophel
net.2SA_16_15: Now when Absalom and all the men of Israel arrived in Jerusalem, Ahithophel was with him.
net.2SA_16_16: When David’s friend Hushai the Arkite came to Absalom, Hushai said to him, “Long live the king! Long live the king!”
net.2SA_16_17: Absalom said to Hushai, “Do you call this loyalty to your friend? Why didn’t you go with your friend?”
net.2SA_16_18: Hushai replied to Absalom, “No, I will be loyal to the one whom the Lord, these people, and all the men of Israel have chosen.
net.2SA_16_19: Moreover, whom should I serve? Should it not be his son? Just as I served your father, so I will serve you.”
net.2SA_16_20: Then Absalom said to Ahithophel, “Give us your advice. What should we do?”
net.2SA_16_21: Ahithophel replied to Absalom, “Sleep with your father’s concubines whom he left to care for the palace. All Israel will hear that you have made yourself repulsive to your father. Then your followers will be motivated to support you.”
net.2SA_16_22: So they pitched a tent for Absalom on the roof, and Absalom slept with his father’s concubines in the sight of all Israel.
net.2SA_16_23: In those days Ahithophel’s advice was considered as valuable as a prophetic revelation. Both David and Absalom highly regarded the advice of Ahithophel.
net.2SA_17_1: Ahithophel said to Absalom, “Let me pick out 12,000 men. Then I will go and pursue David this very night.
net.2SA_17_2: When I catch up with him he will be exhausted and worn out. I will rout him, and the entire army that is with him will flee. I will kill only the king
net.2SA_17_3: and will bring the entire army back to you. In exchange for the life of the man you are seeking, you will get back everyone. The entire army will return unharmed.”
net.2SA_17_4: This seemed like a good idea to Absalom and to all the leaders of Israel.
net.2SA_17_5: But Absalom said, “Call for Hushai the Arkite, and let’s hear what he has to say.”
net.2SA_17_6: So Hushai came to Absalom. Absalom said to him, “Here is what Ahithophel has advised. Should we follow his advice? If not, what would you recommend?”
net.2SA_17_7: Hushai replied to Absalom, “Ahithophel’s advice is not sound this time.”
net.2SA_17_8: Hushai went on to say, “You know your father and his men—they are soldiers and are as dangerous as a bear out in the wild that has been robbed of her cubs. Your father is an experienced soldier; he will not stay overnight with the army.
